questions.question: Why does GitHub require `A`/`AAAA` records for an apex domain (`example.com`) rather than a `CNAME`?
choices: DNS itself disallows `CNAME` records at the apex of a zone, so apex domains must use `A`/`AAAA`
questions.explanation: The DNS specification doesn't allow a `CNAME` to coexist with the other records (SOA, NS) that must exist at a zone apex. Some providers offer 'CNAME flattening' as a workaround, but the portable approach GitHub documents is `A`/`AAAA` for the apex and `CNAME` for subdomains.

questions.question: You want `example.com` to be your canonical hostname and `www.example.com` to redirect to it. Which combination is correct?
choices: DNS: `A` records on apex to GitHub IPs and `CNAME www â†’ your-username.github.io`. `CNAME` file in repo contains `example.com`.
DNS: `CNAME @ â†’ your-username.github.io` and `CNAME www â†’ your-username.github.io`. `CNAME` file in repo contains `www.example.com`.
DNS: `A` records on `www` to GitHub IPs and `CNAME @ â†’ www.example.com`. `CNAME` file in repo contains `example.com`.
DNS: `A` records on both apex and `www` to GitHub IPs. `CNAME` file in repo contains both hostnames, one per line.
questions.explanation: Apex uses `A`/`AAAA` (and optionally `AAAA`) to GitHub's IPs; `www` uses a `CNAME` to `<username>.github.io`. The `CNAME` file in the repo contains exactly one canonical hostname - `example.com` here - and GitHub auto-redirects `www` to it.

questions.question: You're about to configure DNS for the first time. What is the better TTL strategy and why?
choices: Start with TTL 86400 (24h) so caches are warm immediately, then never change it
Start with TTL 300 (5min) so mistakes correct quickly, then raise it once the records are stable
Use TTL 0 to disable caching entirely
Match the TTL to the GitHub Pages cert renewal cycle
questions.explanation: Short TTLs during configuration mean a wrong record corrects itself in minutes rather than a day. Once the records are confirmed stable, raise the TTL to reduce DNS query load and improve resolution speed. TTL 0 is technically valid but disables caching everywhere and isn't useful day-to-day.
